purpose: Audit vbtprobuild V7.2.4 slices 1-19 planning, build history, index state, and persistence mechanics, then compare them against Wave 1-11 crack-repair governance to identify the progression logic that should be preserved in the repo-agnostic workflow.

source_refs.slice_range_start: fec41388 Activate V7.2.4 slice 00 control plane
source_refs.slice_range_end: 5d4dd0f Align Slice 19 report with uncompleted step semantic

core_conclusion.summary: Slices 1-19 built the V7.2.4 capability ladder and discovered the control-plane problems. Wave 1-11 was the governance repair layer that distilled those discoveries into stricter authority classification, conditional generated refresh, read-only validation, and evidence-only report handling.
core_conclusion.slice_era_strength: The slice chain made capability progression explicit: foundation, semantics, grammar, validators, catalogs, provider authorization, bounded source materialization, normalization, features, masks, labels, PIT lineage, VBT sweep compilation, bounded sweep budget, local model config proposals, dry-run dispatch, runtime research, replay/portfolio smoke, and final planning-control evidence.
core_conclusion.slice_era_weakness: The slice chain still carried too much status machinery: per-slice receipts/checkpoints, prompt/handoff artifacts, broad index refreshes, metadata noise, historical next_action leakage, mutable validators, and index freshness ambiguity.
core_conclusion.wave_era_progression: Wave 1-11 kept the useful build discipline but hardened it into governance: one active plan, evidence distinct from authority, focused validators/tests as proof, generated refresh only under explicit triggers, receipts only for writers/closeout, and stale scanner/index findings treated as evidence unless a real validator/test/safety failure exists.

active_plan_lifecycle.during_slice_audit: The plan-note status inside the 2026-05-11 full slice audit reported one active plan: v7_2_4_slice_01_19_v2_addition_overlay_polish_20260507.
active_plan_lifecycle.after_wave_1_11: The same plan is now status=superseded, authority_class=historical_non_authoritative, active_eligible=false, superseded_by=v7_2_4_vbtprobuild_crack_repair_execution_plan_20260513.
active_plan_lifecycle.historical_controller_rule: The older build controller was already demoted to historical reference and explicitly barred from providing next_action, current_decision, handoff state, prompt authority, or slice execution authority.

global_rules_from_slice_plan: Runtime values must be config-owned, not hard-coded.
Completed slices are not rebuilt unless a required validator fails.
Slice 09 and later must consume provider role bridge authority where provider data roles are involved.
Autonomous protected mutation without validation is forbidden.
Future slices use thin V7 wrappers around existing runners.
Production candidate and production-ready claims are forbidden.

shared_authority_model.rule: Slices 12-19 should add thin V7 plans, configs, schemas, validators, receipts, checkpoints, and run-specs around existing active runners instead of creating new hard-run engines by default.

slice_overlay_index.slice_19_semantic: Slice 19 was an uncompleted baseline/planning-control-plane step, not an extension boundary, gate, ceiling, production-readiness proof, or future-work prerequisite.

git_history_shape.interpretation: The slice era was not a clean one-commit-per-slice chain. It was a build ladder with frequent repairs, index refreshes, prompt/context fixes, guidance-map edits, and sidecar proof work. That history explains why Wave 1-11 later had to formalize commit boundaries and generated-refresh triggers.

plan_note_index_during_slice_audit.current_direction: Only failed required tests or validators block next-slice movement; historical controllers are not active authority.

repo_index_observation.slice_audit_problem: The 2026-05-11 slice audit found repo_index stale=false but exact path lookups missing committed Slice 18 surfaces.
repo_index_observation.current_repo_index_summary: repo_index_summary.json declares authority=false and treats projections as discovery/migration inputs, not source authority.
repo_index_observation.progression: This became Wave 8, Wave 10, and Wave 11 logic: exact-path and generated-index misses are routing evidence unless tied to a required proof failure; refreshes need explicit ownership or closeout/future-discovery triggers.

output_schema_observation.interpretation: Slice 19 was indexed as a planning-control artifact, not runtime authority.

good_patterns_to_keep: Single active plan superseded historical controller next_action.
Runtime-affecting values were supposed to be config-owned.
Completed slices were not rebuilt without failed validators.
Existing V2/V4/VBTPro surfaces were reused through thin wrappers and bridge rules.
Receipts and checkpoints preserved execution evidence, output paths, and handoff state.
Tmp reports were allowed as audit evidence when cited by active plan, receipt, checkpoint, or operator request.
problems_that_triggered_later_governance: Per-slice receipts and checkpoints became routine status surfaces, not only proof for writers or closeout gates.
Old prompts, handoff maps, and historical controller fields could still appear in search and confuse next-action authority.
Plan-note metadata was missing for many historical records, causing broad audit noise.
Repo-index stale=false did not guarantee exact-path discoverability.
Some validators could write outputs by default, mixing proof with mutation.
Strict JSON and metric semantics issues appeared in durable Slice 18 outputs.
Sidecar ML/Solthera work and Slice 17 runtime proof increased the need for clear authority boundaries.

what_wave_1_11_preserved_from_slices_1_1: Use one repo-native active plan as the current authority.
Prefer config, schema, contract, validator, and focused tests over chat-only reasoning.
Keep production-ready and production-candidate claims forbidden unless explicit evidence routes satisfy them.
Use existing helper/routing/index surfaces before creating new engines.
Classify historical controllers and tmp reports as evidence instead of next-action authority.
what_wave_1_11_repaired_or_tightened: Plan-note authority classification became config-driven and explicit: active, implemented, superseded, historical, archive_review, evidence_only.
Generated refresh stopped being habitual and became conditional on ownership, stale discovery blockers, future-wave dependency, closeout gates, or operator request.
Focused validators/tests became the slice proof; repo-index status became discovery evidence.
Read-only command harness separated validation from output mutation.
Receipts/checkpoints were reserved for writers, generators, materializers, external actions, irreversible decisions, and real closeout gates.
Head-only staleness after generated refresh was not chased.
Scanner, exact-path, handoff, and superseded-plan findings became evidence unless paired with a required validator/test/safety failure.
wave_1_11_comparison.main_difference: Slices 1-19 were capability-first with growing governance overlays. Wave 1-11 was governance-first and turned those overlays into durable build law.

progression_logic.meaning: Build capabilities in dependency order: claim boundaries before provider pulls, data before features, features before labels/masks, PIT lineage before runtime, runtime before replay, and evidence report before future extension.

As the repo grew, broad prompts, historical controllers, tmp reports, indexes, receipts, and sidecar proof surfaces created routing ambiguity. The repo responded with active overlay plans, guidance maps, active-only context helpers, script policies, and evidence-only tmp classification.

The repo converted observed ambiguity into explicit governance: active plan supersession, authority classification, worktree noise policy, source acquisition semantics, runtime/research mode taxonomy, helper boundary consolidation, read-only harness, and generated-output refresh discipline.

The portable workflow should not copy vbtprobuild prompts or domain policies. It should copy the progression rule: capability slices produce evidence; governance waves distill repeated confusion into packet fields, validators, indexes, and commit-boundary rules.

repo_agnostic_rules_to_persist: Use slices to build small capability bundles and waves only when repeated slice friction exposes a cross-cutting governance problem.
A slice packet must declare the owner bundle, proof commands, boundary rules, refresh decision, and commit plan before protected edits.
Completed slice evidence should be queryable, but only active plan/packet authority should control next action.
Per-slice receipts should not be automatic. Require them only when the slice owns a writer, generator, materializer, external action, irreversible decision, or closeout gate.
Generated indexes are discovery surfaces. They need structured refresh triggers and must not be treated as behavioral proof by default.
Historical plans, prompts, handoff notes, and tmp reports must be classified so they can be found for audits without becoming current instructions.
Read-only validators should be read-only by default; write/regenerate modes need explicit flags and receipts.
The workflow should preserve Codex reasoning by saving decisions into plans, packets, registers, contracts, validators, reports, receipts, and checkpoints, not by copying full prompts as authority.
audit_result: The progression is capability-first slices followed by governance-first repair waves. Slices 1-19 show what the system learned to build; Wave 1-11 shows what the system learned to control.
